Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd study in bursts whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Most of my studying happened late at night or on weekends when I could actually focus without distractions. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often practice problems or work through examples rather than just reading. It was chaotic but somehow it worked for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then other weeks I'd cram like crazy before a deadline. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule because life kept getting in the way, and honestly, I just wasn't motivated to be consistent. If there wasn't immediate pressure, I'd drift and do other things instead.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment notification would make me log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about something due and that would push me to check the LMS. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay updated on my own initiative. It was always reactive, never proactive.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became part of my daily routine. I'd go weeks without logging in and then suddenly be on there multiple times in one day when something was urgent. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of interesting and got me to log in a few extra times, but it still didn't stick as a habit.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful because I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. I watched a lot of them, honestly more than my attendance would suggest. Practice exercises where I could actually do something hands-on felt more useful than just reading. Those matched how I actually learn best.
Q6: I ignored most of the discussion forums and a lot of the supplementary readings. They felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grades. I also skipped a lot of the optional resources because I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff. If it wasn't going to be on an exam, I probably didn't prioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If it was interactive or hands-on, even better. I also valued things that were efficient, like if I could learn something in 20 minutes instead of an hour. Anything that felt like busywork got ignored.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had consequences, so I'd force myself to do them even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff just fell to the bottom of my priority list and usually stayed there. I know that's not ideal, but I had to manage my energy somehow.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them on my own time without being connected to the LMS. I'd print out notes or save PDFs and work through them at a coffee shop or just lying on my bed. I also used YouTube videos and other external sites to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened offline. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then work through problems on paper for hours. I'd also study with friends sometimes, talking through concepts, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere. The LMS only captured a tiny fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd download lecture slides or videos once and then never open them on the LMS again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without internet issues or distractions from notifications. My offline study time was way more than what the system would show.
Q12: There were definitely times when I just couldn't engage. When my stress levels were high or I was dealing with personal stuff, the course felt impossible to care about. Sometimes the material felt too abstract and I'd lose interest quickly. My attendance dropped a lot during those periods even though I was still trying to keep up in my own way.
Q13: Definitely. When I had multiple deadlines from different courses, this one often got deprioritized. Fatigue was a big factor too, especially since I study late at night. Sometimes I was just too mentally exhausted to engage meaningfully with anything, so I'd do the bare minimum or nothing at all.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just feeling drained. I'd scroll through readings without actually reading them. Those sessions felt like a waste of time even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I procrastinated on assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety about not doing them perfectly, and part of it was just not feeling motivated enough. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then suddenly it would be the night before. It's a bad habit I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd cram everything into the last possible moment, which was stressful but somehow I still managed to produce good work. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going and made me feel like my effort was worth it. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and not know how to improve. Quick, specific feedback definitely made me more likely to engage with the next assignment.
Q18: The course structure was okay but it didn't really push me to stay consistent. Having everything available at once meant I could procrastinate easily. If there had been more frequent small deadlines or check-ins, I might have stayed more engaged throughout. The flexibility was both a blessing and a curse.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made things a bit more interesting and gave me small wins to feel good about. But overall, the course felt very self-directed, which is hard for someone like me who drifts without external structure. More interactive or hands-on components would have helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand what's expected without actually completing work. They could be anxious about assignments and keep checking materials without feeling ready to submit. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through even when they're trying.
Q21: That's basically me. I didn't log in much or complete many tasks on the LMS, but I studied a lot on my own and did really well on exams and assignments. The LMS activity doesn't capture offline studying, external resources, or natural test-taking ability. Some people just learn differently than the system expects.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is actually learning or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work, group study, and mental effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it deeply. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. For me, engagement happens when I'm actively working through problems and making connections, even if that doesn't happen on the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned or how hard I worked outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my stress levels, my late-night study sessions, or the fact that I genuinely understood the material despite my low attendance. My grades tell a different story than my login frequency.
